The persist (fix_prompt_repo.ts) is ON CONFLICT (review_id) so the ROW is deduped, but the GitHub comment is NOT. Under the Phase-2 crash → re-run-from-scratch model, a review that crashed AFTER the fix-prompt post (e.g. during ANALYZED/finalize bookkeeping) re-runs orchestrate(), re-reaches posting.ts's fix_prompt block, and posts a SECOND '🔧 Fix-it prompt' comment to the same PR. The post_review_results path is protected by the posted_reviews ON CONFLICT (pr_id) claim + hidden marker; the fix-prompt path has no equivalent. This is the exact gap the plan L70 names.

hazards.title: Paid fix-prompt LLM call re-bills on every re-run with no idempotency_key
hazards.detail: buildFixPrompt (fix_prompt_theme_activity.ts:167) calls client.invokeModel({purpose:'fix_prompt', installationId, ...}) with NO idempotency_key/call_id. On re-run the cross-cutting-themes call is re-issued and re-billed (and re-counted against the per-org cost cap). The Phase-2 in-flight LLM ledger (ADR-0068, plan L59/L68/L73) is the dedupe mechanism, but the fix-prompt invokeModel must supply a STABLE content-addressable key (review_id + included findings) to replay instead of re-pay. It is fail-open so a crash mid-call still ships the deterministic base — but the SUCCESSFUL paid call on attempt N is not memoized for attempt N+1.

hazards.title: No abort re-check before the GitHub post — orphaned handler can post after lease loss
hazards.detail: The fix-prompt activity receives no AbortSignal today; createIssueComment is fired inside a bare try/catch guarded only by `github_installation_id !== null`. Per the plan's Phase-2 HARD CONTRACT (L73), a handler that overran the runtime ceiling keeps running orphaned while another pod has already reclaimed the job. Without threading `signal` into the post + re-checking `signal.aborted` immediately before createIssueComment, a stolen review can still post a fix-prompt comment to GitHub. The DB fence does NOT cover this external write.

hazards.title: No test covers the activity's persist+post orchestration — only the repo and the LLM-themes path
hazards.detail: There is a FixPromptRepo integration test (DB upsert idempotency proven) and a fix_prompt_theme_activity unit test (LLM/deterministic builder), but NO test for generate_fix_prompt.activity.ts::generateFixPrompt — i.e. nothing asserts the persist+post sequence, the fail-open post swallow, OR (critically) re-run post-dedup. Phase 2 adds new re-run-safety behavior to an untested integration seam.

hazards.title: Fix-prompt runs INSIDE the post stage but the post-review claim is a SEPARATE claim — divergent idempotency boundaries
hazards.detail: post_review_results claims posted_reviews keyed on pr_id; the fix-prompt is keyed on review_id (different PK, different table, different claim). A re-run that LOSES the post-review claim (another run already posted) still proceeds into the fix_prompt block in posting.ts because the fix-prompt block's only guard is `aggregated.findings.length > 0`. So the fix-prompt post-dedup cannot piggyback on the post-review claim; it needs its own review_id-keyed fence (plan L70), and that fence must be evaluated even when the post-review path took the lost-claim branch.

maps.provingStrategy:
Add an integration test `test/integration/activities/generate_fix_prompt.activity.integration.test.ts` (mirroring the post_review_results integration tests) against a disposable Postgres (localhost:5433/5434, NEVER the cluster — per the isolated-test-DB memory) with a RECORDING fake FixPromptIssueCommentClient and a fake LlmClientCache that counts invokeModel calls. Scenario discharging the four gates:
1. Run generateFixPrompt twice with the SAME GenerateFixPromptInputV1 (same review_id) — simulating a crash-then-re-run.
2. ASSERT post idempotency (gate 3): the recording client received createIssueComment EXACTLY ONCE across both runs (the review_id-keyed DB fence / marker-lookup suppressed the 2nd post); exactly one physical core.fix_prompts row (the existing repo test already proves the upsert, this adds the POST-claim assertion).

A complementary unit test in test/unit can assert renderFixPromptComment embeds the review_id marker (if shape (a) is chosen) so listIssueComments-based dedup has an anchor.
openQuestions: Post-dedup shape: marker-in-comment + listIssueComments lookup (lighter, no migration, but an extra GitHub GET per review and racy under concurrent re-runs) vs a new core.fix_prompts.github_comment_id/comment_posted_at column updated under an ON-CONFLICT-fenced claim (Pattern-D migration on an existing table, but a real DB fence). Plan L70 says 'DB-fenced claim ON CONFLICT(review_id)' which points at the column approach — confirm whether a 0036+ migration adding the post-state column is in Phase-2 scope.

idempotency_key derivation for the fix-prompt invokeModel: review_id alone, or review_id + a content hash of the included (severity-truncated) findings? The themes synthesis input is the deterministic base prompt, which is a pure function of the findings — so a content hash is replay-stable, but if the findings differ across re-runs (non-deterministic aggregation) the key must still be stable to dedupe the PAID call. Confirm the ADR-0068 ledger key contract and whether fix-prompt should reuse the same call_id scheme as reviewChunk.

Does the shell pass the AbortSignal all the way into posting.ts? orchestrate()'s signature (ReviewPipelineContext) has no signal field today; threading abort to the fix-prompt post requires either adding signal to the context or to the ports — confirm which seam the Phase-2 shell uses so the fix-prompt activity can re-check signal.aborted before the POST.

Ordering vs post-review claim: should the fix-prompt post be SUPPRESSED entirely when the post-review path took the lost-claim branch (another run already owns this PR's review), or should it independently dedupe on review_id? If two distinct review_ids race on the same PR (supersede), do we want one fix-prompt comment per review_id or one per PR? The post-review path is pr_id-keyed; the fix-prompt is review_id-keyed — clarify the desired GitHub-surface behavior under supersede.

maps.subsystem: Supersede + the PR mutex (lease ownership / current_run_id authority / supersede checkpoints / mapFailure→terminal-cancelled) — how the non-Temporal review-job shell must enforce supersede WITHOUT Temporal signals/cancellation.
maps.currentShape:
Today supersede is enforced by FOUR cooperating layers, all orchestrated AROUND `orchestrate()` by the Temporal workflow body `apps/backend/src/workflows/review_pull_request.workflow.ts::reviewPullRequest`:

1. PR MUTEX LEASE (ownership clock). `apps/backend/src/concurrency/pr_mutex.ts` — `acquirePrReviewMutex` (pg_try_advisory_xact_lock + FOR UPDATE on `core.pr_review_mutex` + INSERT minting `mutex_id`; reclaims an expired/NULL lease by marking the old row `released_at`), `renewPrReviewMutexLease` (UPDATE lease_expires_at WHERE released_at IS NULL → returns `rowCount===1`; `false` = LOST CLAIM), `releasePrReviewMutex` (idempotent UPDATE released_at), `withMutexTransaction`. DB `now()` is the single lease authority (`DEFAULT_LEASE_TTL_SECONDS = 30*60`). The mutex is ACQUIRED by the gate activity `apps/backend/src/activities/start_review_for_webhook.activity.ts::startReviewForWebhook` (returns `status='accepted'` + `mutex_id`; `skipped_busy` when a live lease holds it), held for the whole chain, and RELEASED in the workflow's non-cancellable finally.

2. current_run_id AUTHORITY (AD-4 pointer). `apps/backend/src/ingest/_review_run_allocator.ts::allocateRun` composes: `supersedeRun` → INSERT new PENDING run (uuid7) → `flipCurrentRun` → emit WEBHOOK_RECEIVED, all in one tx. `apps/backend/src/ingest/_reviews_repository.ts::flipCurrentRun` atomically flips `core.pull_request_reviews.current_run_id` with an optimistic `oldRunIdExpected` guard (`CurrentRunMismatch`). `apps/backend/src/workflow/_supersede.ts::supersedeRun` cancels the active run (UPDATE review_runs SET lifecycle_state='CANCELLED', cancel_reason, superseded_by_run_id WHERE lifecycle_state IN PENDING/RUNNING/WAITING_RETRY) + emits RUN_SUPERSEDED. `CANCEL_REASONS` includes 'superseded','timeout','shutdown', etc.

3. SUPERSEDE CHECKPOINTS (cooperative abort). The workflow body builds `abortIfClaimLost` (→ `claimStillHeld` → `renewPrReviewMutexLeaseActivity(mutexId)`; renewal ERROR is fail-open returning true, a clean `false` throws `ApplicationFailure.nonRetryable('PrMutexLostClaim')`) and threads it as `ctx.claimCheck`. `apps/backend/src/review/pipeline/orchestrator.ts` fires `ctx.claimCheck()` at FOUR boundaries: before clone (~L360), before classify (~L442), post-classify advisory (~L474), before aggregate (~L656), and a hardening 4th immediately before the post stage (~L849, FIX #10 — closes the steal-then-double-post window).

4. STALE-WRITE GUARD (write-block at the durable boundary). `apps/backend/src/domain/stale_write_guard.ts::assertCurrentRun` (FOR SHARE on pull_request_reviews; throws `StaleWriteError` + emits STALE_WRITE_BLOCKED audit row + queues `codemaster_review_runs_stale_write_blocked_total` when `current_run_id !== runId`). Called by the writers: `post_review_results.activity.ts` (Phase-1 atomic claim, then `INSERT INTO core.posted_reviews ... ON CONFLICT (pr_id) DO NOTHING` — the GitHub-post dedupe), `outbox_dispatch.activity.ts`, `record_review_lifecycle.activity.ts`, `domain/repos/review_findings_repo.ts`.

There is NO symbol literally named `mapFailure` in apps/backend. The "mapFailure → terminal-cancelled (never re-enqueue)" behavior is realized by: (a) the workflow catch block (`review_pull_request.workflow.ts` ~L866-908) which routes `isCancellation(exc)` → `recordRunCancelled` and everything else → `recordRunFailed`, both best-effort, then re-throws; (b) `_supersede.StateDrift`/`StaleWriteError`/`CurrentRunMismatch`/`PrMutexLostClaim` being raised non-retryably so Temporal does NOT redrive; (c) `transitionRun` (`domain/transition_run.ts`) raising `StateDrift` when the run already drifted (e.g. CANCELLED by supersede). In the Phase-1 runner, the analogue knobs are `run_with_retry.ts::RetryPolicy.nonRetryable(e)` and `review_jobs_repo.ts::markFailed` (`state='dead'` when attempts>=max) / `claim` (maxed crashes are NOT reclaimed).

LIVENESS CLOCKS today are THREE separate ones: the PR-mutex lease (renew/janitor), the review_runs RUNNING staleness (reaper), and the new `core.review_jobs` lease (Phase-1 runner). `apps/backend/src/activities/mutex_janitor.activity.ts::mutexJanitorActivity` (5-min schedule) sweeps lease-expired pr_review_mutex rows; `apps/backend/src/activities/review_run_reaper.activity.ts::reviewRunReaperActivity` (10-min schedule, `started_at < now() - staleAfter`, default 3600s) flips stale RUNNING runs → CANCELLED.
keyFiles: apps/backend/src/activities/post_review_results.activity.ts::doPost (Phase-1 claim + posted_reviews ON CONFLICT + lost-claim inherited-outcome path)
maps.whatPhase2Changes:
The shell (the pluggable `JobHandler` for `runOneJob`) must replicate everything `reviewPullRequest` does AROUND `orchestrate()`, but with the JOB LEASE as the ONLY liveness clock and POLLING replacing Temporal signals/cancellation:

1. SUPERSEDE WITHOUT TEMPORAL SIGNALS. Today the supersede signal is implicit: a newer push runs `allocateRun` → `supersedeRun` flips the old run to CANCELLED + `flipCurrentRun` moves `current_run_id`, and the running review notices ONLY via `claimCheck` (mutex renew returning false). The shell keeps EXACTLY this design: there is no out-of-band signal to deliver. The shell threads `ctx.claimCheck = async () => { if (!(await renewPrReviewMutexLease(...))) throw <terminal-cancelled> }` and lets the 5 orchestrator checkpoints poll it. The job's own abort `signal` (from `runOneJob`'s `work` AbortController) is wired into the SAME claimCheck so a job whose lease was stolen ALSO sees signal.aborted. Recommended hardening: make `claimCheck` ALSO verify `current_run_id === runId` (via a read; `assertCurrentRun`-style) so supersede is detected even if the mutex renew transiently fails-open.

2. ENQUEUE-SIDE SUPERSEDE. The ingest path that enqueues a `review_jobs` row must run `allocateRun` (supersede old run + flip current_run_id) BEFORE/with `ReviewJobsRepo.enqueue`. The OLD job row for the superseded run should be proactively marked terminal (not left to crash-loop) — but the authoritative cancel remains `supersedeRun` flipping review_runs, and the OLD job's handler aborts at its next checkpoint.

3. mapFailure → terminal-cancelled (NEVER re-enqueue). The shell handler MUST classify the abort/supersede exceptions (`PrMutexLostClaim`, `StaleWriteError`, `StateDrift`, `CurrentRunMismatch`) as NON-RETRYABLE so `runOneJob`/`markFailed` does NOT re-enqueue. Today this is `ApplicationFailure.nonRetryable`; in the runner this maps to `RetryPolicy.nonRetryable(e)` returning true (in `runWithRetry`) AND/OR the handler calling a `markCancelled` (terminal, distinct from `markFailed`'s retry path). A superseded run is a SUCCESSFUL terminal-cancelled outcome — the loser must release its mutex and exit clean, never bounce back to `state='ready'`.

4. REAPER UNIFICATION (ONE liveness clock). The job lease (`core.review_jobs.leased_until` + heartbeat, reaped by `reapCrashLooped` / `claim`'s expired-lease takeover) becomes the single liveness clock. The shell must NOT depend on the review_run_reaper (review_runs RUNNING staleness) NOR the mutex_janitor (pr_review_mutex lease) as INDEPENDENT liveness — instead the job-lease expiry drives both: when a job is reaped/stolen, the shell (or a unified reaper) must also flip the orphaned review_run RUNNING→CANCELLED and release the orphaned mutex. Decision needed (see open questions): either keep mutex_janitor + review_run_reaper as backstops keyed off the SAME job-lease facts, or collapse them into one job-lease-driven reaper. The current 3-clock design (mutex lease 30min, review_run staleAfter 3600s, job lease) MUST be reconciled or they will disagree about liveness.

5. NON-CANCELLABLE CLEANUP. The workflow's `CancellationScope.nonCancellable` finally (release mutex + workspace on every exit) becomes a plain try/finally in the handler — but it must run BEFORE the runner settles the job (markDone/markFailed), and must be abort-resistant (the finally must not be skipped when `work.signal` fires). Mutex release stays idempotent so a janitor double-release is a no-op.

hazards.title: On crash + re-run, the gate re-acquire mints a NEW mutex_id; the lost original leaks until janitor/lease-expiry (30 min)
hazards.detail: `startReviewForWebhook` mints a fresh `mutex_id` per acquire (pr_mutex.ts: INSERT RETURNING gen_random_uuid()). If the shell re-runs the WHOLE review from scratch on crash (ADR-0077 model) and re-invokes the gate, it either (a) sees its OWN still-live lease and gets skipped_busy → the re-run is blocked for up to 30 min, OR (b) the original lease expired and it reclaims, but the in-flight original handler (if not truly dead) is now a zombie writing under a stale mutex_id. The shell must NOT re-run the gate on retry; it must REUSE the original mutex_id (persist it on the job row / review_run) OR make the gate idempotent (reclaim-by-holder). Today Temporal guarantees one workflow execution per run so this never bit; the from-scratch re-run breaks that guarantee.

hazards.title: claimCheck fail-open hides supersede when mutex renew errors transiently
hazards.detail: `review_pull_request.workflow.ts::claimStillHeld` returns `true` on ANY renewal error (catch → true), so a transient DB blip makes a SUPERSEDED run keep going through its checkpoints. In Temporal this was bounded by the 4 checkpoints + the final pre-post check + the stale-write guard at the durable boundary catching it. The shell must KEEP the stale-write guard (`assertCurrentRun`) as the hard backstop because claimCheck alone is fail-open — and should add a current_run_id read into claimCheck so supersede detection is not solely dependent on the fail-open mutex renew.

hazards.title: posted_reviews ON CONFLICT dedupe is keyed on pr_id, not run_id — correct for double-post but the lost-claim NULL-window needs the IN_FLIGHT_WINDOW heuristic
hazards.detail: GitHub-post idempotency lives in `post_review_results.activity.ts`: Phase-1 `assertCurrentRun` (supersede gate) THEN `INSERT core.posted_reviews ON CONFLICT (pr_id) DO NOTHING` (cross-run dedupe). On a from-scratch re-run the second attempt LOSES the claim and reads the existing row: if `github_review_id` is NULL it disambiguates in-flight-vs-degraded purely by ROW AGE (`age_seconds < inFlightWindow` → raise+retry; else → degraded). A from-scratch re-run that races its own prior (still-in-flight) attempt within the window will RAISE PostReviewTransientError and retry — fine under Temporal, but in the shell the re-run IS the recovery, so it must not treat its own prior in-flight row as a live competitor. The window heuristic assumes the prior owner is a DIFFERENT execution; verify it holds when the prior owner is the crashed-self.

hazards.title: Three liveness clocks can disagree (mutex lease 30min vs review_run staleAfter 3600s vs job lease)
hazards.detail: mutex_janitor reclaims pr_review_mutex at lease-expiry (~30min); review_run_reaper cancels RUNNING runs at `started_at < now()-3600s`; the new job lease/heartbeat is much shorter. A job reaped at (say) 2 min leaves the review_run RUNNING for up to 58 more minutes and the mutex held for up to 28 more — during which a NEW push is `skipped_busy` (mutex still live) AND `current_run_id` still points at the dead run. The reaper-unification gate exists precisely to collapse these. Until unified, a crashed review blocks its PR far longer than the job-lease suggests.

hazards.title: STALE_WRITE_BLOCKED forensic row does NOT survive the outer rollback (documented in stale_write_guard.ts)
hazards.detail: The guard emits STALE_WRITE_BLOCKED + queues the counter, but `post_review_results` wraps the guard in a SAVEPOINT and the StaleWriteError propagates to outer rollback, discarding the forensic row (empirically verified note in stale_write_guard.ts L52-66). So a superseded run's blocked write leaves NO durable audit trace on the post path. The shell's observability for 'how often did supersede block a write' relies on the OTel counter which is ALSO dropped on rollback (emitAfterCommit never drains). Phase-2 should decide whether supersede-loss needs a survives-rollback emit on a separate connection.

hazards.title: Non-cancellable cleanup ordering: mutex release must run before the runner settles the job
hazards.detail: The workflow releases mutex+workspace in `CancellationScope.nonCancellable` BEFORE the BF-5/BF-13 terminal transition. In the shell, `runOneJob` settles the job (markDone/markFailed) in its own try/finally and aborts `work.signal` on settle. If the handler's mutex-release is wired AFTER the work-signal abort or skipped on abort, a superseded/crashed run can leave its mutex held → next push skipped_busy. The handler's finally must release the (idempotent) mutex unconditionally, independent of the abort signal, mirroring the nonCancellable scope.

hazards.title: markFailed re-enqueues by default; supersede/claim-loss must route to a terminal-cancelled path, not the retry path
hazards.detail: `review_jobs_repo.markFailed` sets `state='ready'` (re-enqueue with backoff) unless attempts>=max. If the shell calls markFailed on a `PrMutexLostClaim`/`StaleWriteError`, the superseded job BOUNCES BACK and re-runs — wasting Bedrock and racing the winner. The shell needs either a `markCancelled` (terminal, non-counting) repo method or must classify these exceptions through `RetryPolicy.nonRetryable` so the runner treats them as terminal. There is no such terminal-cancelled outcome in the Phase-1 `RunOutcome` union ('idle'|'done'|'failed'|'lease_lost') yet.

maps.provingStrategy:
Author an integration test against a disposable Postgres (localhost:5433 ONLY per MEMORY — NEVER the cluster) that drives the shell handler directly, colocated next to the modules (repo convention is `*.test.ts` beside source). Three scenarios discharge the four gates:

SCENARIO A — supersede-without-signals + never-re-enqueue (the headline gate): seed a review with run R1 holding mutex M1 + current_run_id=R1; start the shell handler for R1 and pause it at the before-aggregate orchestrator checkpoint (inject a `claimCheck` that yields). Concurrently run `allocateRun` for R2 (which calls `supersedeRun` → R1 CANCELLED + `flipCurrentRun` current_run_id=R2) and re-acquire mutex as M2. Resume R1. ASSERT: (1) R1's next `claimCheck` (renewPrReviewMutexLease(M1) → false) throws PrMutexLostClaim; (2) R1's handler outcome is TERMINAL-CANCELLED — the `core.review_jobs` row for R1 ends `state='dead'`/cancelled, NOT `state='ready'` (never re-enqueued) — assert via `ReviewJobsRepo.getById`; (3) R1 posts NOTHING to GitHub: drive R1 PAST the checkpoint to the post stage and assert `assertCurrentRun` throws StaleWriteError (current_run_id=R2) AND `core.posted_reviews` has no row from R1 / the ON CONFLICT claim is not won by R1; (4) R1 released M1 in its finally (the original row is `released_at NOT NULL`).

SCENARIO B — post-review idempotency across from-scratch re-run (proves gate 3): let R1 win the posted_reviews claim and publish github_review_id=G; then re-run the SAME R1 handler from scratch (crash-recovery). ASSERT the second run LOSES the ON CONFLICT claim, reads the existing row, dispatches `updateReview` (idempotent body refresh) NOT a second `createReview`, and returns the INHERITED publication_outcome — i.e. GitHub is posted exactly once. Use the existing GH client cassette; assert createReview called once total across both runs.

SCENARIO C — reaper unification (proves gate 4): mark R1's job lease expired (set `leased_until` in the past) and crash the handler. Run the unified job-lease-driven reaper (or `reapCrashLooped` + the review_run_reaper + mutex_janitor as wired in Phase-2). ASSERT a SINGLE liveness pass leaves: job row terminal, review_run R1 RUNNING→CANCELLED, AND mutex M1 released — all keyed off the job-lease fact, with no 30-min/3600s lag where a new push to the PR is still `skipped_busy`. Drive a fresh `startReviewForWebhook` immediately after the reaper and assert it returns `accepted` (not `skipped_busy`).

These map 1:1 to the four Phase-2 gates: A=supersede-without-Temporal + mapFailure→terminal-cancelled, A(3)+B=post idempotency / abort-aware side-effect contract (no external write after supersede), C=reaper unification.
openQuestions: ADR-0077 is NOT present on disk in this worktree (docs/adr/ stops at 0076; no temporal-removal/job-runner ADR found by content search). The mapping is grounded entirely in the Phase-1 runner code + the frozen Temporal workflow. Confirm where ADR-0077 lives and whether it specifies mutex_id reuse-on-rerun vs gate-idempotency — this is the single biggest unresolved design point.

On a from-scratch re-run, does the shell RE-INVOKE the gate (`startReviewForWebhook`, minting a new mutex_id) or REUSE the original mutex_id persisted on the review_run/review_jobs row? The gate is built to be called once per chain; re-invoking it either self-blocks (skipped_busy on own live lease) or reclaims (zombie risk). The runner currently has no place that persists mutex_id onto the job row (`ReviewJobsRepo.enqueue` does not store it).

Should the three liveness clocks (mutex lease 30min, review_run staleAfter 3600s, job lease) be COLLAPSED into one job-lease-driven reaper, or kept as independent backstops? If kept, what reconciles the 3600s review_run lag vs a sub-minute job lease so a crashed review does not block its PR for ~an hour? The reaper-unification gate needs a concrete target topology.

There is no `markCancelled` / terminal-cancelled outcome in the Phase-1 runner (`RunOutcome` = idle|done|failed|lease_lost; `markFailed` re-enqueues until attempts>=max). Does Phase-2 add a distinct terminal-cancelled repo method + RunOutcome, or route supersede via `RetryPolicy.nonRetryable` + markFailed-as-dead? A superseded loser must be a clean terminal, not a 'failure'.

claimCheck is fail-open on mutex-renew error. Should Phase-2 strengthen it to ALSO read `current_run_id === runId` (fail-closed on supersede) so detection does not depend solely on the fail-open renew? The stale-write guard backstops the durable path but the orchestrator does up-front non-durable work (clone, Bedrock chunk calls = paid) before any guarded write.

Does the posted_reviews IN_FLIGHT_WINDOW heuristic (age-based NULL-row disambiguation in post_review_results.activity.ts) hold when the 'prior owner' of the NULL row is the CRASHED SELF being recovered, rather than a concurrent different execution? A from-scratch re-run within the window will RAISE+retry against its own in-flight row.

Where does the supersede-loss observability live once Temporal is gone? STALE_WRITE_BLOCKED rows + the OTel counter are both dropped on the outer rollback of the post path (documented), so the shell may have no durable signal of how often supersede blocked a write.

maps.subsystem: Reapers + liveness — the two-clock unification (review_run_reaper age-sweep vs review_jobs lease) + mutex_janitor
maps.currentShape:
THREE independent liveness clocks exist today, all on this branch:

CLOCK 1 — AGE-BASED run reaper (the one to retire). `reviewRunReaperActivity(deps)` in apps/backend/src/activities/review_run_reaper.activity.ts:127. Single CTE: `UPDATE core.review_runs SET lifecycle_state='CANCELLED', cancelled_at=now(), cancel_reason='timeout' WHERE lifecycle_state='RUNNING' AND started_at < now() - make_interval(secs => $1) RETURNING run_id, review_id` then LEFT-JOINs `pull_request_reviews → repositories` to resolve installation_id for the audit emit (orphan = NULL installation_id → reaped, audit skipped). The threshold $1 = resolveStaleAfterSeconds(deps): `deps.staleAfterSeconds` || env CODEMASTER_REVIEW_RUN_REAPER_STALE_AFTER_SECONDS || DEFAULT_STALE_AFTER_SECONDS=3600, then Math.max(.,MIN_STALE_AFTER_SECONDS=300). The liveness signal is `review_runs.started_at` WALL AGE — there is NO lease, NO heartbeat, NO worker-identity column on core.review_runs (schema 0001_baseline.sql:2000; columns run_id/review_id/lifecycle_state/started_at/cancelled_at/cancel_reason; cancel_reason CHECK allows only superseded|operator_cancelled|timeout|repository_disabled|installation_suspended|shutdown). Fired by reviewRunReaperWorkflow() (workflows/review_run_reaper.workflow.ts:68, pure pass-through proxy) on the `codemaster-review-run-reaper` Temporal cron `*/10 * * * *` (worker/outbox_dispatcher_main.ts:59-65, WAVE1_LIVENESS_SCHEDULES, overlap=SKIP).

CLOCK 2 — LEASE-BASED job reaper (Phase 1, the survivor). `ReviewJobsRepo.reapCrashLooped()` in apps/backend/src/runner/review_jobs_repo.ts:79: `UPDATE core.review_jobs SET state='dead', dead_reason=COALESCE(.,'lease expired with attempts exhausted (crash loop)'), finished_at=now(), <clears lease meta> WHERE state='leased' AND leased_until < now() AND attempts >= max_attempts`. The liveness signal is `review_jobs.leased_until` (DB-now lease set on claim review_jobs_repo.ts:33, renewed every heartbeatS by runOneJob's heartbeat loop review_job_runner.ts:31-40 via heartbeat() which also fences on attempt_token + timeout_at). Called once per idle cycle by RunnerLoop.run() (review_job_runner.ts:87). claim()'s reclaim branch (review_jobs_repo.ts:38) re-leases `state='leased' AND leased_until < now() AND attempts < max_attempts` — so the SAME leased_until clock drives both re-run (attempts remain) and dead-letter (attempts exhausted).

CLOCK 3 — MUTEX lease janitor (parallel, NOT in scope to retire but coupled). `mutexJanitorActivity(deps)` in apps/backend/src/activities/mutex_janitor.activity.ts:90 sweeps `core.pr_review_mutex WHERE released_at IS NULL AND (lease_expires_at IS NULL OR lease_expires_at < now()) FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`, marks released_at. The mutex lease (pr_review_mutex.lease_expires_at, default 1800s, concurrency/pr_mutex.ts:DEFAULT_LEASE_TTL_SECONDS=30min) is renewed by the Temporal workflow via renewPrReviewMutexLeaseActivity at 3 claim-check boundaries (review_pull_request.workflow.ts:394-412 claimStillHeld/abortIfClaimLost). Fired by mutexJanitorWorkflow() on cron `*/5 * * * *` (outbox_dispatcher_main.ts:52-58).

The Temporal workflow reviewPullRequest (workflows/review_pull_request.workflow.ts:361) is what wraps orchestrate(ctx) at :791 — it owns the run RUNNING→COMPLETED/FAILED/CANCELLED transitions (recordRunFailed/recordRunCancelled/finalizeReviewRun at :818,:875,:893), the mutex acquire(gate)/renew/release, and runLifecycleBookkeeping. There is NO writer of core.review_jobs yet in the live review path (review_jobs_repo.ts:enqueue has no production caller; the run row is created upstream, the job row is Phase-2's new producer).

maps.whatPhase2Changes:
Phase 2 must collapse the two RUN-liveness clocks (CLOCK 1 age-sweep + CLOCK 2 job lease) into ONE = the job lease, so the review-job shell's lease is the sole authority for whether a run is alive. Concretely:

1. SHELL-OWNS-RUN-TRANSITIONS. The non-Temporal shell handler (the JobHandler passed to runOneJob) must replicate what reviewPullRequest does AROUND orchestrate(): acquire/renew/release the pr_review_mutex, flip the run RUNNING→COMPLETED on clean return (finalizeReviewRun), and — critically for unification — flip RUNNING→FAILED/CANCELLED on the shell's OWN terminal paths (markFailed→dead, supersede→cancelled). Today the Temporal workflow's catch block writes recordRunFailed/recordRunCancelled (review_pull_request.workflow.ts:866-908); the shell must do the equivalent so a run does not get orphaned at RUNNING needing the age-reaper to clean it.

2. UNIFY THE REAPER. Replace the age-based review_run_reaper with a job-lease-driven finalizer: when reapCrashLooped dead-letters a job (leased_until<now AND attempts>=max), the SAME sweep (or a folded-in successor) must ALSO flip its FK-anchored core.review_runs row RUNNING→CANCELLED cancel_reason='timeout' (the join review_jobs.run_id → review_runs.run_id is the integrity anchor, 0036 line 4). This is the "fold Phase-1's reapCrashLooped into the unified reaper" item (plan line 72/479).

3. DISABLE THE AGE-SWEEP FOR RUNS WITH A LIVE JOB ROW. Either (a) gut/retire reviewRunReaperWorkflow + its cron schedule (outbox_dispatcher_main.ts:59-65) entirely once every run has a review_jobs row, OR (b) scope its CTE so it only reaps runs with NO live review_jobs row: add `AND NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM core.review_jobs j WHERE j.run_id = review_runs.run_id AND j.state IN ('ready','leased'))` to the WHERE in review_run_reaper.activity.ts:140-155. (b) is the safer staged form during the Phase-4 dual-run window (some runs still Temporal-driven, no job row → age-sweep still governs them); (a) is the end state. The plan's wording "disable the review_run_reaper for runs with a live review_jobs row" maps exactly to option (b)'s NOT EXISTS predicate.

4. MUTEX JANITOR (CLOCK 3) STAYS. It governs a different resource (pr_review_mutex), not run liveness, and the shell still renews the mutex lease around orchestrate(). No unification needed — but the shell's lease (job) and the mutex lease (pr_review_mutex) become two leases the one shell must keep alive in lockstep; if the shell only heartbeats the JOB lease and forgets to renew the MUTEX lease, the janitor steals the mutex mid-review and a superseding review double-spends Bedrock.

hazards.title: Age-sweep cancels a live long-running review out from under the shell (the core unification bug)
hazards.detail: review_run_reaper.activity.ts uses started_at < now()-3600s as the ONLY liveness signal. A legitimately slow review (large PR, many chunks, LLM/GitHub retries, RETRY_POLICIES worst-case ~6min per reviewChunk × N chunks) that exceeds 3600s wall while its job lease is alive and heartbeating WILL be flipped RUNNING→CANCELLED cancel_reason='timeout' by the age-sweep — even though the worker is alive. The shell then finishes and tries finalizeReviewRun RUNNING→COMPLETED, which hits the ck_review_runs_cancelled_at_state / lifecycle CHECK or StateDrift. Until the NOT EXISTS predicate (or schedule retirement) lands, the two clocks actively fight. The 3600s default makes this latent, not theoretical, on big PRs.

hazards.title: Mutex lease (Clock 3) and job lease (Clock 2) can diverge — janitor steals the mutex mid-review → double Bedrock spend
hazards.detail: Today the Temporal workflow renews the pr_review_mutex lease at 3 claim-check boundaries (review_pull_request.workflow.ts:394-412) and the mutex lease default is 30min (pr_mutex.ts:DEFAULT_LEASE_TTL_SECONDS). The shell must renew BOTH the job lease (heartbeatS, runOneJob) AND the mutex lease on its own cadence. If Phase 2 wires the job heartbeat but not the mutex renewal, mutexJanitorActivity sweeps the mutex (lease_expires_at<now) after 30min, a re-push acquires a fresh mutex and starts a 2nd concurrent review of the same PR — the exact cost-safety failure pr_mutex.ts:54-62 warns about. The shell needs the abortIfClaimLost/claimStillHeld lost-claim semantics replicated so a stolen mutex aborts the review.

hazards.title: review_runs has no run_id→review_jobs back-reference for the NOT EXISTS scoping; the join is one-directional
hazards.detail: The integrity anchor is review_jobs.run_id → review_runs.run_id (0036:4). To scope the age-sweep to 'runs WITHOUT a live job row' you must correlated-subquery from review_runs INTO review_jobs (NOT EXISTS SELECT 1 FROM review_jobs WHERE run_id=review_runs.run_id AND state IN ('ready','leased')). There is no index on review_jobs.run_id for the non-partial case — uq_review_jobs_active_run is partial on state IN ('ready','leased') (0036:33), which actually COVERS this exact predicate, so the planner can use it. A Phase-2 author who writes the predicate against a different state set (e.g. forgets 'ready') loses the index and re-introduces the age-sweep racing a job that is enqueued-but-not-yet-claimed.

hazards.title: Folding reapCrashLooped into the unified reaper must also cancel the run, atomically, or runs orphan at RUNNING
hazards.detail: reapCrashLooped (review_jobs_repo.ts:79) only flips the JOB to 'dead'; it does NOT touch core.review_runs. A crash-looped review therefore leaves the job 'dead' but the run stuck 'RUNNING' forever once the age-sweep is disabled for runs-with-a-job-row — the worst outcome of a half-done unification (you removed the age-sweep backstop AND the lease reaper doesn't finalize the run). The unified reaper MUST, in one transaction, dead-letter the job AND flip its FK run RUNNING→CANCELLED (cancel_reason='timeout', cancelled_at=now()), honoring ck_review_runs_cancelled_at_state.

hazards.title: started_at default-on-insert makes the age clock start at run creation, not at work start
hazards.detail: core.review_runs.started_at DEFAULT now() NOT NULL (0001_baseline.sql:2014) — it is stamped at INSERT, not when orchestrate() begins. review_jobs.started_at is COALESCE(started_at, now()) at first claim (review_jobs_repo.ts:34). So the two clocks measure different epochs: the age-sweep counts from run-row creation (which may include queue wait before a worker claims the job), the lease counts from claim. During dual-run this mismatch means the age-sweep is even MORE aggressive than the lease (a job queued 50min then claimed and running 20min is 70min-old by started_at but has a fresh lease). Reinforces hazard #1: the age clock cannot be made equivalent to the lease clock by tuning the threshold; it must be disabled per-run.

hazards.title: Disabling the cron schedule is operator state, not code — ScheduleAlreadyRunning is swallowed
hazards.detail: ensureCronSchedule (ensure_schedule.ts:96) swallows ScheduleAlreadyRunning and never updates an existing schedule (comment ensure_schedule.ts:116-120: 'don't overwrite operator tuning'). So if Phase 2 retires reviewRunReaperWorkflow by removing it from WAVE1_LIVENESS_SCHEDULES, the ALREADY-CREATED codemaster-review-run-reaper schedule in the live Temporal cluster keeps firing — code removal alone does not stop it. Retirement needs an explicit operator step (tctl schedule delete codemaster-review-run-reaper) documented in the cutover runbook, OR the workflow body must be gutted to a no-op so a still-firing schedule does nothing.

maps.provingStrategy:
Two integration tests against the disposable Postgres (per the ADR-0077 plan: :5434/codemaster, describeDb/INTEGRATION_DSN harness — NEVER the shared cluster, per the isolated-test-DB memory). 

GATE TEST 1 (the unification — age-sweep must NOT cancel a run with a live job lease): seed a core.review_runs row in lifecycle_state='RUNNING' with started_at = now()-7200s (2× the 3600 threshold), AND a core.review_jobs row for that run_id in state='leased' with leased_until = now()+60s (live lease). Run the unified reaper (the new scoped reviewRunReaperActivity with the NOT EXISTS predicate, staleAfterSeconds floored 300). ASSERT the run stays lifecycle_state='RUNNING' (reaped=0) — the live job lease shields it. Then flip the job to state='dead' (or delete it) and re-run: ASSERT the run flips to CANCELLED cancel_reason='timeout' (the age-sweep governs ONLY orphan runs). This is the file test/integration/activities/review_run_reaper.activity.integration.test.ts (the plan names it as the mirror harness), extended with the live-job-row scenario.

GATE TEST 2 (fold reapCrashLooped + run cancellation atomic): seed a review + run (RUNNING) + job (state='leased', leased_until=now()-1s, attempts=max_attempts=1) — a crash-looped job. Run the unified reaper. ASSERT BOTH: review_jobs.state='dead' dead_reason LIKE '%crash loop%' AND core.review_runs.lifecycle_state='CANCELLED' cancel_reason='timeout' cancelled_at IS NOT NULL — proving the job dead-letter and the run cancellation happen together (no run orphaned at RUNNING). Pair with a negative: a job state='leased' leased_until=now()+60s (live) leaves both job AND run untouched. This discharges plan-line-72's 'fold Phase-1 reapCrashLooped into the unified reaper' + 'one liveness clock = the job lease'.
openQuestions: Staged-disable (NOT EXISTS predicate, option b) vs hard-retire (delete schedule, option a): during the Phase-4 dual-run window some runs are still Temporal-driven with NO review_jobs row and need the age-sweep — so the NOT EXISTS form must ship FIRST and the hard-retire only after every review path produces a job row. Which window does Phase 2 target?

Does the unified reaper live as a NEW review_jobs-repo method (e.g. reapStuckRuns folding reapCrashLooped + the run-cancel UPDATE) called by RunnerLoop, OR does it stay an activity/cron? If RunnerLoop owns it, the */10 cron + reviewRunReaperWorkflow + its activity all retire; if it stays cron, the schedule must be re-pointed at the unified body. The plan says 'fold into the unified reaper' but doesn't name the host.

The age-sweep's threshold (3600s) is intended as a worst-case-review backstop; once the job lease (default leaseS, renewed by heartbeat) is the authority, is there ANY residual need for a wall-clock ceiling on a run, or does the job-level timeout_at (review_jobs.timeout_at, the hard runtime ceiling set on claim) fully subsume it? If timeout_at subsumes it, the run-level age concept disappears entirely.

Mutex-lease renewal in the shell: does the shell call renewPrReviewMutexLeaseActivity on its own timer (replicating the 3 claim-check boundaries), or does it piggyback the job heartbeat? They have different TTLs (mutex 30min vs job leaseS) — the shell needs an explicit renewal cadence for BOTH or the janitor (Clock 3) steals the mutex. Phase 2 must specify the mutex-renewal seam inside the shell.

Operator cutover: the already-created codemaster-review-run-reaper Temporal schedule survives code removal (ensureCronSchedule swallows ScheduleAlreadyRunning, never deletes). Does Phase 2 gut the workflow body to a no-op (safe under a still-firing schedule) or require a documented tctl schedule delete in the cutover runbook?

maps.subsystem: Cancellation / abort-awareness across the review pipeline's external side effects (git clone, Bedrock/Anthropic LLM, GitHub API fetch+post, static-analysis subprocesses) — the abort-aware side-effect contract the Phase-2 review-job shell must enforce around an UNCHANGED orchestrate().
maps.currentShape:
Cancellation TODAY is Temporal-only; there is NO JS AbortSignal anywhere in the pipeline data path. The wrapper that drives orchestrate() is apps/backend/src/workflows/review_pull_request.workflow.ts::reviewPullRequest. Its only cancellation mechanisms are: (1) Temporal CancellationScope.nonCancellable(...) wrapping the mutex+workspace cleanup-finally (lines ~832-864) and the BF-13 run-transition (lines ~872-907), with isCancellation(exc) routing RUNNING→CANCELLED via recordRunCancelled; (2) the per-PR mutex LEASE check threaded as ctx.claimCheck = abortIfClaimLost (lines ~405-412), which calls renewPrReviewMutexLeaseActivity(mutexId) and throws ApplicationFailure.nonRetryable("PrMutexLostClaim") when the DB says the lease was reclaimed by a newer review. orchestrate() (apps/backend/src/review/pipeline/orchestrator.ts::orchestrate, line 351) fires ctx.claimCheck() at exactly three boundaries (before clone ~360, before classify ~442, before aggregate ~656) — that is the ONLY "abort" awareness orchestrate has, and it is a cooperative lease poll, not a signal.

The activity-port surface ReviewActivityPorts (apps/backend/src/review/pipeline/activity_ports.ts:96) defines every pipeline step (clone, classify, chunkAndRedact, staticAnalysis, reviewChunk, aggregate, postReview, ...) as `method(input: SomeInputV1): Promise<...>` — ZERO methods accept an AbortSignal. So orchestrate() literally cannot pass a signal down even if it had one.

External-call abort-awareness, call site by call site:
- GIT CLONE — apps/backend/src/integrations/git/cloner.ts::GitSubprocessCloner.clone (line 97). Spawns git via node:child_process. Has an INTERNAL 60s timeout + SIGTERM→5s→SIGKILL grace via the transportAbortSignal seam (awaitWithTimeout, line 253). Accepts NO external signal param — its clone() arg object has no `signal`. A job-abort cannot stop an in-flight clone.
- LLM — apps/backend/src/integrations/llm/client.ts::LlmClient.invokeModel (line 344) → this.sdk.createMessage(...) (line 491). The LlmSdk port (line 132) createMessage args carry NO signal; the Bedrock/anthropic SDK call is un-cancellable here. BUT idempotency is already solved: ledger probe is check-FIRST (lines 411-435), keyed via LlmInvocationLedger.computeKey on reviewId+chunkId+role+model+promptSha256+toolSchemaVersion; a HIT replays the stored provider response and SKIPS createMessage + cost-cap + record_call_cost.
- GITHUB FETCH+POST — apps/backend/src/integrations/github/api_client.ts::GitHubApiClient._request (line 387) and FetchGitHubHttpClient.request (line 197). request() arms ONLY transportAbortSignal(this.timeoutMs) (default 10s, line 207); no external signal param on _request/get/post/put/patch/delete. The post path (post_review_results.activity.ts) is therefore un-cancellable mid-POST, but GitHub posting idempotency is already solved by the 2-phase atomic claim (see gate 3 below).
- STATIC ANALYSIS — apps/backend/src/analysis/static_analysis_orchestrator.ts uses an AbortSignal internally (deadlineController.abort() soft-barrier, ~line 174; threaded as RunnerRunInput.signal into in_worker_runner.ts which does process-group SIGTERM/SIGKILL, ~line 159). This is the ONLY subsystem with a signal plumbed end-to-end — but the signal is an internal DEADLINE controller, not an externally-supplied job-abort signal.

Phase-1 runner: apps/backend/src/runner/review_job_runner.ts::runOneJob already MINTS a cooperative abort: `const work = new AbortController()` and passes work.signal to the handler (JobHandler = (job, signal) => Promise<void>, line 14). The hard-runtime ceiling does work.abort("max runtime exceeded") then settles the job as failed via repo.markFailed — but the comment at lines ~50-53 is explicit: "Handler overran the ceiling (and may still be running, orphaned — it violated the honor-signal contract)". So the worker slot returns; the handler keeps running. Today NOTHING consumes that signal because no JobHandler exists yet (grep: JobHandler has zero consumers outside review_job_runner.ts; orchestrate is imported ONLY by the Temporal workflow).
keyFiles: apps/backend/src/workflows/review_pull_request.workflow.ts:reviewPullRequest (the Temporal wrapper the shell replicates AROUND orchestrate — gate/placeholder/allocate, claimCheck=abortIfClaimLost, CancellationScope.nonCancellable cleanup-finally, BF-5/BF-13 run-transition)
apps/backend/src/review/pipeline/orchestrator.ts:orchestrate (line 351; the UNCHANGED core; ctx.claimCheck() at lines ~360/442/656 are the only abort boundaries)

maps.whatPhase2Changes:
The Phase-2 review-job shell is a JobHandler (job, signal) => Promise<void> that replicates everything review_pull_request.workflow.ts::reviewPullRequest does AROUND orchestrate(), MINUS Temporal: gate (startReviewForWebhook) → placeholder → enrich → allocateWorkspace → ANALYSIS_STARTED → build ReviewPipelineContext → orchestrate(ctx) → bookkeeping → ANALYZED → finalize, all bracketed by a non-Temporal try/finally that releases the mutex + workspace on EVERY exit path. Concretely for THIS subsystem the shell must:

(GATE 1 — abort-aware side-effect contract) Thread the Phase-1 `signal: AbortSignal` (from runOneJob's `work`) into orchestrate via a NEW ctx field (e.g. ctx.abortSignal) AND replace ctx.claimCheck with a hybrid that ALSO checks signal.aborted. Then the HARD CONTRACT: (a) make each external call abort-aware by adding an optional `signal` to its boundary — GitSubprocessCloner.clone arg object, FetchGitHubHttpClient.request/GitHubApiClient._request, LlmSdk.createMessage / LlmClient.invokeModel — and combine it with the existing transportAbortSignal via AbortSignal.any([external, transport]); (b) enforce "no external WRITE after signal.aborted" by gating the two write-bearing external calls (LLM createMessage on a MISS, and post_review_results' createReview/updateReview) behind a signal.aborted check at the activity boundary, returning/aborting BEFORE the write rather than after. The cleanup-finally that releases mutex+workspace must run even when aborted (the non-Temporal analogue of CancellationScope.nonCancellable — a plain finally block that ignores the signal, since the runner already returned the slot).

(GATE 2 — LLM ledger) Wire the REAL LlmInvocationLedger into the review LlmClient construction (today the ledger field is OPTIONAL and the Temporal path leaves it undefined). On a crash-rerun the shell re-runs orchestrate from scratch; every per-chunk createMessage probes the content-addressable key and replays instead of re-paying. No ledger code changes needed — only the composition wiring + passing the `idempotency` context (reviewId/chunkId/toolSchemaVersion) at the reviewChunk dispatch, which today the workflow path does not populate.

(GATE 3 — post idempotency) NO change to doPost is needed: the 2-phase claim already dedupes across re-runs (ON CONFLICT (pr_id) DO NOTHING → loser dispatches the idempotent updateReview body-refresh, never a second createReview; the HTML markerFor(prId) makes the body itself idempotent). The shell must simply call orchestrate UNCHANGED so post still routes through post_review_results. The ONE risk to prove: the AD-4 stale-write guard (assertCurrentRun on runId) must not block the SAME job's re-run — Phase-1 re-runs reuse run_id (per the memory "causal correctness over convenient idempotency"), so the guard sees the same run and permits the claim/update.

(GATE 4 — reaper unification) Collapse the THREE liveness clocks to ONE = the job lease. Today: (i) review_jobs lease + attempt_token fence (Phase-1), (ii) pr_review_mutex lease via renewPrReviewMutexLeaseActivity, (iii) review_runs.started_at staleness via review_run_reaper.activity. Phase-2 must make the job lease authoritative: the shell holds the mutex/run-row for exactly the job-lease duration, so a dead worker's job lease expiring (reapCrashLooped / claim-reclaim) is the single signal that releases the mutex and flips the run terminal — retiring or subordinating reviewRunReaperActivity and the mutex-lease renewal so they cannot independently reap a still-live job (or fail to reap a dead one) on a DIFFERENT clock than the job lease.

hazards.title: No-post-after-abort is UNENFORCEABLE today — every external write call ignores the abort signal
hazards.detail: runOneJob's hard-timeout does work.abort() but the handler 'may still be running, orphaned' (review_job_runner.ts ~line 50). The orphaned handler can still call LlmClient.invokeModel→sdk.createMessage (paid write) and post_review_results→ghClient.createReview (GitHub write) AFTER the abort, because neither boundary accepts a signal (api_client.ts:197/387 arm only transportAbortSignal; client.ts:491 createMessage has no signal; cloner.ts:97 clone has no signal). The HARD CONTRACT 'no external write after signal.aborted' therefore cannot be satisfied without adding a signal param + a pre-write signal.aborted gate at each of these three boundaries. This is the central Phase-2 deliverable, not a detail.

hazards.title: Three independent liveness clocks can disagree — a still-live job can be reaped, or a dead one left RUNNING
hazards.detail: review_run_reaper.activity.ts reaps review_runs where started_at < now()-stale (default 3600s, floor 300s) — a clock totally decoupled from the job lease (review_jobs.leased_until / heartbeat_at) and from the mutex lease. A slow-but-live Phase-2 job whose review_run crosses 3600s gets CANCELLED out from under it by the reaper even though its job lease is healthy; conversely a job that dies just after claim leaves the review_run RUNNING for up to an hour while its job lease expired in seconds. Gate 4 requires the job lease to be the SOLE authority; until the reaper + mutex-lease are subordinated to it, the system has split-brain liveness.

hazards.title: Cloner timeout and post-stage are un-cancellable; a long clone/post burns the hard-runtime ceiling and orphans
hazards.detail: GitSubprocessCloner.awaitWithTimeout enforces its OWN 60s + 5s+5s kill grace independent of the job's maxRuntimeS. A clone that hangs near the job's hard ceiling cannot be shortened by the job abort (no external signal reaches it), so runOneJob's hard-timeout fires, returns the slot, and leaves the git subprocess + askpass token-script on disk (cloner.ts removes the askpass script only in its own finally, line 168 — which never runs if the process is orphaned). The shell must thread the job signal into clone() and rely on the existing SIGTERM/SIGKILL teardown to actually kill the subprocess on abort.

hazards.title: LLM ledger is wired OPTIONAL and the Temporal path leaves it undefined — gate 2 is latent, not active
hazards.detail: LlmClient.ledger is `LlmInvocationLedgerPort | undefined` (client.ts:301) and the idempotency context is OPTIONAL on invokeModel (line 365). The comment is explicit: 'Platform jobs / unit tests leave it undefined' and 'When absent ... the client behaves exactly as the frozen Python (invoke, no replay)'. So unless the Phase-2 composition root constructs the review LlmClient WITH LlmInvocationLedger.fromDsn AND the reviewChunk dispatch populates {reviewId, chunkId, toolSchemaVersion}, a crash-rerun re-pays for EVERY chunk. The mechanism exists; the wiring is the gate.

hazards.title: AD-4 stale-write guard could block the same job's legitimate re-run if run_id is not stable across re-runs
hazards.detail: doPost Phase-1 runs assertCurrentRun(runId, reviewId) inside a SAVEPOINT before the ON CONFLICT claim (post_review_results.activity.ts ~line 951); a StaleWriteError rolls back the claim. Gate 3's idempotency depends on a re-run presenting the SAME run_id so it is recognized as the current run (then loses the claim → updateReview). If the Phase-2 re-run mints a NEW run_id, the guard treats the prior run as superseded and the path diverges. The runner's review_jobs row carries run_id (ReviewJobsRepo.enqueue) — the shell MUST pass job.run_id into orchestrate, not a fresh one.

hazards.title: The cleanup-finally is Temporal-CancellationScope-shaped; the non-Temporal shell must replicate 'runs even under abort' semantics by hand
hazards.detail: The mutex+workspace release (review_pull_request.workflow.ts ~832) and the BF-5/BF-13 run-transition (~872) both rely on CancellationScope.nonCancellable so they execute before the CancelledFailure re-propagates. In the non-Temporal shell a plain try/finally suffices ONLY because the runner already returned the slot (the handler is not forcibly killed mid-finally). But if the shell ever runs cleanup via an awaited external call (releasePrReviewMutexActivity, releaseWorkspace) that itself respects the abort signal, the cleanup could be skipped on abort. Cleanup writes must be deliberately EXEMPT from the abort signal (the 'no write after abort' rule applies to REVIEW side effects, not to lease/mutex RELEASE).

maps.provingStrategy:
Author a co-located integration test for the Phase-2 shell handler (mirror the runner's existing test placement — the chaos test from commit 1129707 already proves lease-steal/fence). Concrete scenarios, each discharging one gate:

GATE 1 (abort-aware + no-post-after-abort): Drive the shell handler with a stub ReviewActivityPorts whose reviewChunk and postReview record every invocation, plus an AbortController. Abort the signal AFTER clone+classify but BEFORE the post stage; assert (a) ctx.claimCheck/abort boundary short-circuits orchestrate so postReview is NEVER called after signal.aborted (spy.callCount stays 0 on postReview), and (b) the stub LLM/post clients each RECEIVE the threaded signal (assert the arg object carries `signal` === the aborted signal). A SECOND assertion: with a slow stub clone that resolves AFTER abort, assert the git stub observed signal.aborted and tore down (no checkout call recorded). This is the falsifiable proof of 'no external write after abort'.

GATE 2 (ledger replay): Use an in-memory LlmInvocationLedgerPort fake. Run the shell once to completion (records N createMessage calls), then re-run the SAME job (same run_id/review_id/chunk_ids); assert createMessage call count stays N (every chunk replayed), costCap.checkOrRaise/recordCallCost NOT called on the second run, and the returned findings byte-identical.

GATE 3 (post idempotency) — this needs a disposable Postgres (localhost:5433 throwaway ONLY per the isolated-test-DB memory, never the cluster): run doPost twice for the same pr_id via the shell; assert exactly ONE core.posted_reviews row, the second call takes the lost-claim branch and dispatches updateReview (not a second createReview), and the review body carries a single markerFor(prId).

GATE 4 (one liveness clock): assert that with the reaper subordinated, a job whose lease is healthy but whose review_run started_at exceeds the old 3600s threshold is NOT reaped (the reaper either no-ops or reads the job lease), and a job whose lease expired flips both the review_run terminal AND releases the mutex via the SAME job-lease-expiry path (reapCrashLooped/claim-reclaim), proving a single authority.
openQuestions: ADR-0077 (cited as the source of truth, 'also on this branch') is NOT present on feat/de-temporal-runner-phase1 — the highest ADR is 0076 (helm-chart). The runner files are also named differently than the task brief (review_job_runner.ts / review_jobs_repo.ts, not the named symbols). Confirm whether ADR-0077 lives on a different branch or is still to be authored — the Phase-2 plan's 'reuse orchestrate() UNCHANGED inside the shell' contract should be pinned to it.

Does the Phase-2 shell add `signal` to the shared ReviewActivityPorts type (touching all 25+ methods + every proxy/stub) OR introduce a separate non-Temporal port bundle? The Temporal workflow uses proxyActivities (network boundary) where a JS AbortSignal does NOT cross to the activity worker; the in-process shell calls activities as plain functions where it DOES. This dual-runtime asymmetry means the SAME ports type may need different abort semantics per runtime — needs an explicit design decision.

Should reviewRunReaperActivity be DELETED (its review_runs staleness clock fully replaced by the job lease) or RE-BASED to read the job lease? It is currently fired by a Temporal Schedule (review_run_reaper.workflow.ts via ensureCronSchedule). De-Temporalizing the runner means the reaper's own Temporal-Schedule trigger also needs a non-Temporal home (a cron/runner sweep).

The mutex lease (renewPrReviewMutexLeaseActivity, default TTL from CODEMASTER_*_LEASE) and the job lease (review_jobs.leased_until) currently renew on DIFFERENT cadences. For gate 4 to hold, must the mutex lease TTL be derived from / pinned to the job lease, or should the shell stop renewing the mutex independently and let the job-lease-expiry release it? Needs the mutex-lifecycle owner's sign-off (ADR-0064 mutex-liveness territory).

Is the LLM createMessage genuinely un-cancellable at the SDK layer (anthropic[bedrock]), or does the @anthropic-ai/sdk client accept a `signal`/`AbortSignal` in its request options? The LlmSdk port (client.ts:132) hides this. If the SDK supports abort, gate 1 for the LLM write is plumbing; if not, the only enforcement is the pre-call signal.aborted gate (no in-flight cancel of a paid call already on the wire).
